perturbation.original_text: If either Company decides that it does not wish to proceed with a Possible Transaction, it will promptly inform the other Company of that decision. In that case, or at any time upon the request of a Provider for any reason, a Recipient will, and will cause its Representatives to, within five business days of receipt of such notice, destroy or return all Evaluation Material, except for that portion of notes, analyses, compilations, studies, interpretations or other documents or records prepared by the Recipient or its Representatives which does not contain in any respect any of the Evaluation Materials, in any way relating to the Provider or its products, services, employees or other assets or liabilities, and no copy or extract thereof (including electronic copies) shall be retained, except that Recipient’s outside legal counsel may retain one copy to be kept confidential and used solely for the purpose of establishing Recipient’s compliance with its obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: If either Company decides that it does not wish to proceed with a Possible Transaction, it will promptly inform the other Company of that decision. In that case, or at any time upon the request of a Provider for any reason, a Recipient will, and will cause its Representatives to, within one business day of receipt of such notice, destroy or return all Evaluation Material, except for that portion of notes, analyses, compilations, studies, interpretations or other documents or records prepared by the Recipient or its Representatives which does not contain in any respect any of the Evaluation Materials, in any way relating to the Provider or its products, services, employees or other assets or liabilities, and no copy or extract thereof (including electronic copies) shall be retained, except that Recipient’s outside legal counsel may retain one copy to be kept confidential and used solely for the purpose of establishing Recipient’s compliance with its obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return/destroy deadline from five business days to one business day introduces a potential conflict with reasonable business practices and legal expectations for document retrieval and destruction, making it potentially non-compliant with standards of fairness.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP Rule 34(b)(2)(A) requires a party to respond to a request for production within 30 days after being served. While this rule is about responding to a request, the general implied expectation is a 'reasonable time' to comply with discovery requests. Forcing a company to destroy all evaluation material within one business day of notice is a deviation from what is considered a reasonable time frame, potentially making it difficult to ensure proper and complete compliance and introducing the potential for spoliation claims if information is not properly handled.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 34. Producing Documents, Electronically Stored Information, and Tangible Things, or Entering onto Land, for Inspection and Other Purposes | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 34. Producing Documents, Electronically Stored Information, and Tangible Things, or Entering onto Land, for Inspection and Other Purposes (a) In General. A party may serve on any other party a request within the scope of Rule 26(b) : (1) to produce and permit the requesting party or its representative to inspect, copy, test, or sample the following items in the responding party's possession, custody, or control: (A) any designated documents or electronically stored information—including writings, drawings, graphs, charts, photographs, sound recordings, images, and other data or data compilations—stored in any medium from which information can be obtained either directly or, if necessary, after translation by the responding party into a reasonably usable form; or (B) any designated tangible things; or (2) to permit entry onto designated land or other property possessed or controlled by the responding party, so that the requesting party may inspect, measure, survey, photograph, test, or sample the property or any designated object or operation on it. (b) Procedure. (1) Contents of the Request. The request: (A) must describe with reasonable particularity each item or category of items to be inspected; (B) must specify a reasonable time, place, and manner for the inspection and for performing the related acts; and (C) may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (2) Responses and Objections. (A) Time to Respond. The party to whom the request is directed must respond in writing within 30 days after being served or — if the request was delivered under Rule 26(d)(2) — within 30 days after the parties’ first Rule 26(f) conference. A shorter or longer time may be s

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text shortens the deadline for destroying evaluation material from five business days to one. The Law Explanation suggests this creates a potential conflict, as FRCP Rule 34(b)(2)(A) implies a 'reasonable time' for compliance which one day may not be. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that a request must "specify a reasonable time, place, and manner for the inspection and for performing the related acts". While this snippet refers to production of documents, not destruction, the Perturbation Explanation argues this also creates a potential problem with fairness and introduces a potential for spoliation if information is not properly handled. Because of this contradiction, the response is YES.

perturbation.original_text: Neither Recipient will, within one year from the date of this Agreement, directly or indirectly solicit the employment or consulting services of or employ or engage as a consultant any of the officers or employees of the Provider, so long as they are employed by the Provider and for three months after they cease to be employed by Provider.
perturbation.changed_text: Neither Recipient will, within two years from the date of this Agreement, directly or indirectly solicit the employment or consulting services of or employ or engage as a consultant any of the officers or employees of the Provider, so long as they are employed by the Provider and for three months after they cease to be employed by Provider.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to two years introduces a potential restraint of trade issue, making it harder for employees to seek new opportunities and potentially conflicting with state laws that limit the duration of non-solicitation agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits contracts that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. While non-solicitation agreements are sometimes upheld, a two-year restriction could be seen as an unreasonable restraint, especially in California, making it unenforceable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract extends the non-solicitation period from one year to two years. According to the Law Explanation, this extension could be seen as an unreasonable restraint of trade, particularly in California. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." While non-solicitation agreements are sometimes upheld, the increased duration introduces a significant risk of being deemed an unenforceable restraint under California law, constituting a legal contradiction because it potentially violates this statute.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall terminate two years after the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall terminate six months after the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the agreement's term to six months could create issues regarding the protection of trade secrets, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws that protect such information for a longer period if reasonable measures are taken to maintain secrecy.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) protects trade secrets for as long as they remain secret and the owner takes reasonable steps to maintain their secrecy. Reducing the term of the agreement to six months might not provide sufficient protection for trade secrets that require a longer period to remain confidential, especially if the information is highly sensitive. This creates a conflict with the intent of the UTSA to provide ongoing protection for valuable trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The change in the contract, reducing the termination period from two years to six months, contradicts the intent of trade secret law, specifically the UTSA as explained by the LLM. The LLM explains that the UTSA protects trade secrets as long as they remain secret and reasonable steps are taken to maintain secrecy. Reducing the agreement's term to six months may not provide sufficient protection, especially if the information requires a longer period to remain confidential. The provided law snippet 2, while a 'page not found' error from the Uniform Law Commission, still reinforces that the ULC deals with providing states with legislation that brings clarity and stability to critical areas of state statutory law, which includes trade secrets. Therefore, reducing the time conflicts with maintaining secrecy, a core tenet of trade secret protection.
